title: Monitoring & Observability
description: Monitoring and observability are how you understand what your systems are doing. Monitoring tells you something is wrong; observability lets you ask why. At FAANG, every service emits metrics, logs, and traces. This guide covers the Linux-native tooling for system monitoring and the concepts that scale from single-server debugging to fleet-wide observability.

sections.title: The Three Pillars: Metrics, Logs, Traces
sections.description: Metrics are numeric measurements (CPU, memory, requests per second, error rates) — best for alerting and dashboards. Logs are discrete events with timestamps — best for debugging specific incidents. Traces follow a request across services — best for understanding distributed system behavior. At FAANG, the 'three pillars' are served by specialized systems: Prometheus (metrics), Elasticsearch/Splunk (logs), Jaeger (traces). On a single Linux system, the same concepts apply with local tools.
sections.codeBlock:
# === CPU Metrics ===
cat /proc/stat                         # Overall CPU stats (user, nice, system, idle, iowait, irq, softirq, steal)
mpstat -P ALL 1                        # Per-CPU breakdown
sar -u 1 5                             # Historical CPU stats (from sysstat package)

# === Memory Metrics ===
cat /proc/meminfo                      # Detailed memory state
free -h                                # Quick summary
sar -r 1 5                             # Historical memory stats

# === Disk Metrics ===
iostat -x 1 5                          # Device-level I/O metrics
sar -b 1 5                             # Historical I/O stats
sar -d 1 5                             # Per-device I/O stats

# === Network Metrics ===
sar -n DEV 1 5                         # Per-interface bandwidth
sar -n TCP,ETCP 1 5                    # TCP stats (connections, retransmits, segments)
ip -s link                             # Interface errors, drops, overruns

# === System Load ===
cat /proc/loadavg                      # Load average (1/5/15 min)
# Load is NOT CPU % — it's the number of processes in running + uninterruptible state
# On an 8-core system: load 8 = fully utilized (each core has 1 task)

# === Top/htop ===
top -b -n 1 | head -20                 # Batch mode (non-interactive)
htop                                   # Interactive with better UX

# === Collect all metrics with a script ===
cat > quick_health.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "=== UPTIME ==="
uptime
echo "=== CPU ==="
grep 'cpu ' /proc/stat | awk '{usage=($2+$4)*100/($2+$4+$5)} END {print usage "%"}'
echo "=== MEMORY ==="
free -h | grep Mem
echo "=== DISK ==="
df -h / /var/lib /data 2>/dev/null
echo "=== TOP PROCESSES ==="
ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -6
echo "=== NETWORK ==="
ip -s link | grep -E "(eth|ens|enp)" -A 2
EOF

sections.title: Prometheus & Node Exporter
sections.description: Prometheus is the industry standard for metric collection and alerting. It scrapes HTTP endpoints and stores time-series data. Node Exporter exposes Linux system metrics in Prometheus format. At FAANG, Prometheus is the default metrics system — every service exposes a /metrics endpoint, and node_exporter runs on every server for hardware/OS metrics.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Node Exporter ===
# Install:
sudo apt install prometheus-node-exporter
# Or download from https://prometheus.io/download/

# Node exporter exposes metrics at :9100/metrics
# Key metric groups:
# node_cpu_seconds_total     — CPU time by mode
# node_memory_MemTotal_bytes — Memory stats
# node_disk_io_time_seconds_total — Disk I/O
# node_network_receive_bytes_total — Network
# node_filesystem_avail_bytes — Disk space
# node_load1, node_load5, node_load15 — Load averages

# === Check node exporter metrics ===
curl -s localhost:9100/metrics | head -30

# === PromQL (Prometheus Query Language) examples ===
# CPU utilization (1m average):
# 100 - (avg by(instance) (rate(node_cpu_seconds_total{mode="idle"}[1m])) * 100)

# Memory utilization:
# (1 - node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes / node_memory_MemTotal_bytes) * 100

# Disk space > 80%:
# node_filesystem_avail_bytes{mountpoint="/"} / node_filesystem_size_bytes{mountpoint="/"} < 0.2

# === Prometheus configuration (prometheus.yml) ===
# scrape_configs:
#   - job_name: 'node'
#     static_configs:
#       - targets: ['localhost:9100', 'server1:9100', 'server2:9100']

# === Installation ===
sudo apt install prometheus
# Web UI at localhost:9090

# === Alertmanager rules ===
# groups:
#   - name: node_alerts
#     rules:
#       - alert: DiskSpaceLow
#         expr: node_filesystem_avail_bytes{mountpoint="/"} / node_filesystem_size_bytes{mountpoint="/"} < 0.1
#         for: 5m
#         labels: { severity: critical }
#         annotations: { summary: "Disk space < 10% on {{ $labels.instance }}" }

sections.title: Logging: journald, syslog & Logrotate
sections.description: Linux has two logging systems: the legacy syslog (text files in /var/log/) and the modern journald (binary, structured). At FAANG, logs are shipped to centralized systems (Splunk, Elasticsearch, Datadog) — but the local management of logs (rotation, size limits, compression) is still critical to prevent filling the disk.
sections.codeBlock:
# === journald ===
# Centralized logging with structured metadata
# Config: /etc/systemd/journald.conf

# Key settings:
# SystemMaxUse=1G        — Limit total journal disk usage
# MaxRetentionSec=2week  — Auto-delete old logs
# Compress=yes           — Compress old journal files
# ForwardToSyslog=no     — Don't duplicate to syslog

# === logrotate ===
# Rotate, compress, and delete old log files
# Config: /etc/logrotate.conf + /etc/logrotate.d/

# Example: /etc/logrotate.d/nginx
/var/log/nginx/*.log {
    daily
    missingok
    rotate 14           # Keep 14 days
    compress
    delaycompress       # Don't compress newest file
    notifempty
    create 0640 www-data adm
    sharedscripts
    postrotate
        systemctl reload nginx  # Signal nginx to reopen logs
    endscript
}

# Test logrotate:
sudo logrotate -d /etc/logrotate.d/nginx  # Dry-run (debug)
sudo logrotate -f /etc/logrotate.d/nginx  # Force run

# === /var/log/ key files ===
# /var/log/syslog          — General system log (Debian/Ubuntu)
# /var/log/messages        — General system log (RHEL/CentOS)
# /var/log/auth.log        — Authentication logs (SSH, sudo)
# /var/log/kern.log        — Kernel messages
# /var/log/dmesg           — Kernel ring buffer
# /var/log/nginx/          — Web server logs
# /var/log/mysql/          — Database logs

# === Centralized logging ===
# rsyslog — forward logs to central server
# /etc/rsyslog.conf:
# *.* @logserver.example.com:514     # Send all logs via UDP
# *.* @@logserver.example.com:514    # Send all logs via TCP (reliable)
# Use TLS for production:
# *.* @@logserver.example.com:6514

# === Log format for machine parsing ===
# JSON logs are preferred at FAANG (easier to parse)
# Example: logger '{"event":"deploy","service":"myapp","status":"ok"}'

# === Structured logging in applications ===
# KEY=value format for grep-ability:
# 2024-01-15T10:30:00 [INFO] user_id=abc123 action=login duration_ms=42

# Journald structured fields:
journalctl -o verbose | grep -E "(MESSAGE=|SYSLOG_IDENTIFIER=|_PID=)"

sections.title: Distributed Tracing & Observability Concepts
sections.description: On a single server, you track a request through the OS. In a distributed system, you track it across services. Distributed tracing propagates a trace ID across service boundaries, allowing you to reconstruct the full path of a request. At FAANG, tracing is a first-class concern — every framework and infrastructure component propagates trace context.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Single-server tracing (low-level) ===
strace -f -e trace=network -p <PID>     # Trace network syscalls
perf trace -p <PID>                     # Trace syscalls with perf

# === OpenTelemetry (industry standard) ===
# OpenTelemetry provides:
# - APIs and SDKs for generating traces, metrics, logs
# - Exporters to send data to backends (Jaeger, Prometheus, Datadog)
# - Context propagation (W3C TraceContext, Baggage)

# === Trace context propagation ===
# Trace ID is passed via HTTP headers:
# traceparent: 00-0af7651916cd43dd8448eb211c80319c-b7ad6b7169203331-01
#   version-trace_id-span_id-trace_flags

# === Key metrics from tracing ===
# Latency: time spent in each service
# Error rate: failed requests / total requests
# Saturation: utilization of each service
# Apdex: user satisfaction score based on latency thresholds

# === RED Method (for service monitoring) ===
# Rate: requests per second
# Errors: failed requests per second
# Duration: latency (average, p50, p95, p99)

# === USE Method (for resource monitoring) ===
# Utilization: % busy
# Saturation: queue length
# Errors: error count

# === Four Golden Signals (Google SRE) ===
# 1. Latency: time to serve a request
# 2. Traffic: demand on the system
# 3. Errors: rate of failed requests
# 4. Saturation: how 'full' the system is

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'You're on call and get paged for high CPU on a production server. Walk through your response.'

A: 'Acknowledge the page. SSH in (or use session manager). Run uptime to see load averages — is it a spike or sustained? mpstat -P ALL 1 — is one core pegged or all cores? ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head — which process is using the CPU? perf top -p <PID> — which function is hot? If it's a known pattern (e.g., GC in Java, V8 garbage collection in Node), check the application logs. If it's a new pattern, capture a perf record and create a flamegraph. Check if a deploy just happened — rollback if it's a regression. If the process is legitimately busy (traffic spike), add capacity (horizontal scaling) or check if upstream throttling was removed. File a postmortem ticket.'

Q: 'How do you set up monitoring for a new Linux service?'

A: 'First, identify the four golden signals. Install node_exporter to collect OS-level metrics. Add a /metrics endpoint to the application exposing RED metrics (rate, errors, duration). Set up Prometheus to scrape both. Create a Grafana dashboard with: (1) request rate and latency, (2) error rate by status code, (3) CPU/memory/disk of the service process, (4) database connection pool usage if applicable. Set alerts for: p99 latency > 500ms for 5 minutes, error rate > 1% for 5 minutes, disk > 80% full. Test the alerts by simulating the failure conditions. At FAANG, every service has a 'service level objective' (SLO) — a target for availability or latency — and monitoring measures against that SLO.'

Q: 'What's the difference between monitoring and observability?'

A: 'Monitoring is tracking known failure modes — CPU > 90%, disk full, process not running. It answers 'what is broken?' Observability is the ability to ask arbitrary questions about your system without knowing the answers in advance — 'why is this specific request slow?', 'what is this process waiting for?'. Monitoring requires predefined dashboards and alerts. Observability requires structured data (traces, structured logs, high-cardinality metrics) and tools to explore it. At FAANG, we invest heavily in observability because the most impactful outages are rarely the things you thought to monitor in advance.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Prometheus Documentation — Official Prometheus docs and best practices
  • OpenTelemetry Documentation — Industry standard for observability
  • Google SRE Book: Monitoring — The four golden signals and monitoring philosophy

details:
FAANG Architect's Note: The most important skill in monitoring is knowing what to monitor. At FAANG, we don't monitor everything — we monitor the things that tell us whether the system is healthy. For each service, define: 'If this metric goes bad, will I be woken up at 3 AM?' If not, don't alert on it. Put it on a dashboard if it's useful for debugging, but don't page people for things they can't act on. The best monitoring setup is the one where pages are rare (because the system is stable) and actionable (because when a page comes, you know exactly what to do).

Production Tip: Never alert on average latency. Average hides outliers — a 1% error rate and 99% good requests can still have a 'good' average. Always alert on percentiles: p99 latency (the worst 1% of requests) and error rate. At FAANG, we monitor p50 (typical), p95 (slow), p99 (very slow), and p99.9 (outliers). A request that takes 10 seconds for 0.1% of users can be invisible in the average but devastating for those users. Set your SLOs on p99, build dashboards on p50, and debug with p99.9.
